chat.author: Ondřej Caletka
chat.text: @Rudiger: there was a proposal to add some more explanation about what can possibly go wrong by Maria. But it seems that WG is not interested in elaborating more on this topic: https://mailarchive.ietf.org/arch/msg/sidrops/FotQhXB7t-XsRMD2hGAj3VfdS4o/

chat.author: David Lamparter
chat.text: (The audio problem is room echo I believe, I believe it's very hard to understand remote questions from the front since the speakers are aimed at the room -> poor ratio of direct speaker audio vs. echo from back of room wall)

chat.author: David Lamparter
chat.text: The CZnic/RIPE "mishap" does feel like a bit of a terminology (or rather semantics) issue

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Job misrepresented RRDP's behavior again.  Incremental downloads go from where you are to current, unless you're so out of date that there is no incremental download available and you have to do full transfer..

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: (We seem to have this conversation at every meeting :( )

chat.author: Tim Bruijnzeels
chat.text:
Fallback to snapshot in RRDP can be problematic, e.g. in cases of low bandwidth where clients do not succeed in downloading deltas - and then fall back to snapshots. Also the fallback between RRDP and rsync leads to full resyncs. And RRDP session resets on the server side also lead to full resyncs.
The erik protocol has the advantage that is relies on a canocal representation of the repository (based on the total set of current manifests), and this means in short that resynchronisation can be done more cheaply. And since the client can build this representation locally - it also means that e.g. going from an RRDP snapshot, or local rsync cache to an Erik relay does not result in a full resync. Similarly being bounced from one Erik relay to another does not result in full resyncs. The short of it (and sorry for the long message) clients can work out efficiently if there are any newer manifests, and if there are any new files listed on those. This allows for mirroring as well.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: @Rob Austein client's don't know the content (the set of the signed objects) until they've downloaded them, this means that if the RP switches between RRDP and Rsync and back to RRDP again, it will do a full snapshot download the last 'back to RRDP again'. In RRDP you cannot skip downloading any deltas even though those deltas contain data the RP already possesses

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: I wouldn’t do the locpref thing myself but ISTM it’s different to see something as a side effect than it is to explicitly signal validation state. (I think the locpref thing is the former.)

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: At the bottom of that slippery slope otherwise, I can say that if I withdraw a notfound route, that was a form of signaling the rpki state in the protocol. :-o

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: @job I suppose that if your implementation randomly flails between rsync and rrdp it might get that confused.  I don't know why one would choose to implement it that way (my implementation certainly didn't).

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Fun? :-P

chat.author: Russ Housley
chat.text: How does this align with 6489?

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: @Rob Austein the remote repository might have an operational issue with their webserver, but Rsync still up, all modern implementations will try RRDP first, and if that doesn't work (HTTP != 200) then try Rsync. But upon a later point in time they'll try RRDP again

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: Did rpki.net not fall back to rsync if RRDP was unavailable?

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: (example: for a while I had to email APNIC R&D every 3 months because their letsencrypt crontab wasn't working properly :smile:)

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Yes, of course.  But if you start generating your examples of what happens when one protocol fails while the other works you need to perform the same experiment  with what happens while flipping back and forth between rsync, rrdp, and erik.   Hint: I doubt it would be any prettier.   In general, if you assume randomly failing servers you are going to have efficiency problems at least, you're doing well if you can just avoid correctness problems.  NB: I don't object to erik, I object to disingenuous examples of why what you want to replace is broken.

chat.author: Tim Bruijnzeels
chat.text: I think we can explain all those scenarios but not in a 10 minute presentation

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: My point is that RRDP failures are common place, so we need to design accordingly. The failure path in RRDP just is more expensive: it's "download the snapshot", while in Rsync, the failure path is cheaper: it's "fetch the delta". In Erik it's also cheaper: it's "fetch the delta based on merkle tree"

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: there is nothing disingenuous here

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Then you need to explain it better when you present that slide at every meeting :)

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: and worse: once a bunch of clients decide to download the snapshot, they can overload the repository server, which in turn might cause disruptions for other clients, causing those clients to also start downloading the snapshot... in this sense in a bandwidth constraint environment (read: server behind congested network) rsync performs better because it transfers the delta (and calculates the delta on the fly, so it is an implicit "resume" functionality) and Erik has that same property, and RRDP does not

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: yes, i wish I had an hour to explain the intricate details :-D

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Wasn't the entire point of RRDP that we could use commericla web caching services to avoid overload problems?  Yes if you underprovision your server you can get into trouble, with any protocol.

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Having seen Erik presented at least twice now, couldn’t you skip the motivation next time and establish detente that way?

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: i asked for 15 minutes and got 10 minutes...

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: improvize adapt overcome

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: @Rob Austein on the server side you might use a fancy CDN, but that doesn't resolve the problem that RRDP transmits more data than strictly needed: it is full history replication, so when later events cancel out earlier events, client's still download both. So this means that on the _client_ side more data is consumed than needed, a CDN doesn't solve that, and the CDN bill is higher than it needs to be...

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: point being: server being under provisioned is a problem, and can be solved with CDN sometimes, but clients might also be under provisioned, and that is harder to solve, only solution is more efficient protocol (hopefully erik is that, but measurements will tell)

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: No, RRDP does not replay the entire history, as long as you just use RRDP.

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Deltas are always from what client last saw to current state, anything that came and went during that interval goes unmentioned completely.  It's when you flail between protocols or are too far behind that you start getting inefficiencies.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text:
delta 0: ROA A is added
delta 1: ROA B is added
delta 2: ROA B is removed
delta 3: ROA C is added
When client is at delta 0, it wants to jump to delta 3, it MUST download delta 1 & 2 - even though delta 2 cancels out delta 1. This is the full history replication aspect

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: You must be reading a different protocol document from the one Tim and I wrote, because that's not what it says.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text:
RFC 8182 Section 3.4.2

If an Update Notification File contains a contiguous chain of links to Delta Files from the last processed serial number to the current serial number, then Relying Parties MUST attempt to download and process all Delta Files in order of serial number as follows.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: in other words: the client cannot avoid downloading delta 1 & 2 in my example, because it does not know what delta 1 & 2 contain until after it has downloaded those.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: When I say "full history is replicated", it is a different way of saying "client must download all deltas" (full history since the client latched on to a session)

chat.author: Mingqing(Michael) Huang
chat.text: correct me if I am wrong--  Erik relay still needs rsyc/rrdp talk to PP, so it seems the main goal is time saving. I am wondering: what cost it will require to insert a new Erik relay layer to the current architecture  ? given that it itself can introduce complexity and communication layer--which also require time-consuming.  another question jump in my mind is -- who will be qualified to provider Erik service? it seem get more concentrated, not as distributed as regular.

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: My bad, I was remembering semantics like DNS AXFR and rpki-rtr..  I'm pretty sure that's what I proposed for RRDP too, don't remember it changing.  Sigh.

chat.author: Tim Bruijnzeels
chat.text: In a way the erik approach allows a client to 'self-heal' cheaply. It can work out the difference between its state and the server's (erik relay) state locally - based on index and partition files that are published by the relay (i.e. they are pre-calculated and can be cached and the server does not need to invest cpu en mem). This also means that bouncing between relays is not costly (e.g. if the other relay is behind then the client knows the info is irrelevant - and if it is ahead it can just get the diff only). This make mirroring feasible.

chat.author: Job Snijders
Job Snijders
chat.text: so - yes, the RRDP server will "cancel out and hide" events that overtake each other, where possible with the information the RRDP server had available at the time. But my observation is that in practice "unfortunate timing" often leads to lack of ability for the server to aggregate events that cancel each other out.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: My apologies for being unable to explain my observations into fewer words!

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: (i do think we now have a better mutual understanding of why we use the words we use?)

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Thank you for your patience with my mis-remembering of how the deltas work in RRDP, it's fundamentally different from the other protocols I mentioned and presumably I once knew that since I implemented the silly thing back in the day.  The differences in how the deltas work are small but significant in this context.  So you are right and I was wrong.

chat.author: Tim Bruijnzeels
chat.text: fwiw I am happy to document fail-over behaviours between rrdp, rsync and erik (but will likely need to find the time for it first) - it's good to make that clear.

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Sounds worthwhile

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: I think we both displayed a fair degree of patience carrying this conversation forward from meeting to meeting :-) All good. It has been quite amazing to analyse RRDP and learn about data replication. Originally I thought the inefficiency I thought I saw in RRDP was caused by the XML encoding, but that was a total red herring. HTTP Gzip compression totally eradicates the XML/Base64 overhead

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: Maybe we should just follow Geoff's suggestion from more than a decade ago and just use git :)

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: this has been tried and does not scale to the levels we need here: steady state has 500,000+ objects, of which about 180,000 changes every day. Github will close your account if you have a repo that's so large and churning that much (Ben Maddison experienced this when he was storing RPKI data in git on github :smile:)

chat.author: Rob Austein
chat.text: git <> github, but fair point.

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: other issue with git: it is not stateless, it requires client & server to have a common history, and "history" is exactly what's not super useful in RPKI, RPs only need "latest"

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: (some other people suggested to use Git instead of Erik.. so i took a look at if it could work, but concluded it wouldn't be an improvement on RRDP)

chat.author: Job Snijders
chat.text: this presentation about timing / convergence is awesome
